preFix.title: Cutting and fitting stair carpet with gripper rods
preFix.subtitle: Lay carpet on a straight flight using gripper rods and an underlay
preFix.excerpt: How to fit carpet on a straight staircase using gripper rods and foam underlay, covering how to cut the carpet to width, fix the grippers, fit the underlay to each tread and riser, and press the carpet home with a knee-kicker and bolster.
preFix.sourceNotes: British Carpet Manufacturers Association guidelines. City and Guilds Level 2 Flooring. Carpet Foundation installation guidance (open-access technical notes). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: Fitting stair carpet on a straight flight is a one-person job that takes most of a day. The
covered here is the most common domestic approach: the carpet flows over each nose in a continuous piece, held at each tread-riser junction by a
. A winding staircase or any stair with curved or tapered treads requires a professional cut and fit; the straight-flight method described here is not transferable to those shapes.

Stair carpet, cut to the stair width plus 25 mm each side (the carpet tucks down the side gap in the tread). Stair underlay (foam or sponge, cut to fit each tread only, not the riser). Gripper rods, one per tread plus one at the top landing (the riser-side gripper is optional for waterfall fitting). 13 mm round-head nails for the grippers (if using nail-down grippers) or adhesive gripper for concrete stairs. Carpet tape for the underlay. A bolster chisel for tucking the carpet edges.
Step 1, measure and cut the carpet

Measure the total run of the stairs: from the top landing edge, down the riser, across the tread, down the next riser, and so on to the bottom. Add 100 mm for trimming. Cut the carpet to this length from the roll; the width is the stair width plus 50 mm total. Cut cleanly on the back of the carpet with the utility knife against a straight batten. Lay the cut piece pile-side down and mark the tread depth plus riser height as a fold guide; this helps when cutting the underlay.

Fix a gripper rod at the back of each tread, with the pins pointing toward the riser (not toward the front of the tread). Position the gripper so its back edge is approximately 10 mm from the riser face; this gap lets the carpet stretch onto the pins without the riser face pressing the carpet off them. For a timber staircase, nail the grippers with the 13 mm round-head nails provided. On a concrete stair, use the adhesive pad on the back of the gripper or a grip adhesive. Fix a gripper on the top landing at the head of the stairs, pins pointing toward the stair.
Step 3, cut and fix the underlay

Cut a piece of underlay for each tread only; the riser face has no underlay in the waterfall method. Each underlay piece should fit from the back of the gripper to the front of the tread nose, and across the full tread width. Staple or tape the underlay to each tread, keeping it clear of the gripper pins.
Step 4, lay and fix the carpet

Start at the bottom of the staircase. Tuck the cut end of the carpet between the bottom riser and the floor using the bolster chisel, driving it into the gap neatly. Work upward: smooth the carpet across the first tread and press it firmly onto the gripper pins with the heel of the hand or a knee-kicker, engaging it onto the pins at the riser junction. Work from the centre of the tread outward to each side, avoiding diagonal pulls. Fold the carpet over the tread nose and smooth it down the riser face. Repeat for each step.
Step 5, trim and tuck the sides and top

At each tread, tuck the carpet side edges down into the gap between the carpet and the string (the side board) using the bolster chisel. The 25 mm allowance on each side disappears into this gap. At the top landing, press the carpet onto the landing gripper pins, trim to length with the utility knife, and tuck the edge under the skirting or landing threshold bar.

Stair carpet wears primarily at the nose of each step. Shifting the carpet 25 to 50 mm up or down the run when this area shows wear (while the tread body is still good) extends carpet life by distributing the wear zone. Leave enough spare carpet at the top or bottom of the run to allow one shift.

attrs.fix: Press the carpet firmly back onto the pins with a knee-kicker or by kneeling on a broad piece of wood and pressing down. If the pins are bent or the gripper has pulled loose from the tread, re-fix the gripper before re-seating the carpet.
attrs.cause: Carpet not pressed firmly enough onto the gripper pins; pins not fully engaging the backing.
attrs.symptom: Carpet lifts away from a tread after a few weeks

attrs.fix: Lift the carpet, trim the underlay so it ends just short of the gripper rod and does not cover the riser face. Re-lay and press the carpet back onto the grippers.
attrs.cause: Underlay cut too wide and overlapping onto the gripper or the riser face.
attrs.symptom: Ridges form across the tread surface under the carpet

attrs.fix: Lift the carpet from the bottom two or three treads, re-tension it using a knee-kicker or by pulling it taut toward the top before pressing onto the pins. The carpet should have a slight upward tension as it seats onto each gripper.
attrs.cause: The carpet was not tensioned onto the grippers; it is lying rather than gripping.
attrs.symptom: Carpet creeps toward the bottom of the stairs over time

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
paragraph reads at grade 11.3 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Fitting stair carpet on a straight flight is a one-person job that takes most of a day. The waterfall method covered here is the most common
